mapping.a_element: lower/upper cutoff frequencies f_low << f_high bounding the scale-free band
mapping.b_element: fastest vs. slowest cascade timescales bounding the multi-scale memory range (e.g. the ~2-4 year slowest volatility component)

mapping.a_element: the 1/f^alpha spectral exponent characterizing the power-law noise
mapping.b_element: the persistence/antipersistence memory exponents estimated by the variance ratio

mapping.a_element: turbulence-like intermittency explaining deviations from pure 1/f (alpha != 1)
mapping.b_element: the empirically-fitted volatility cascade with multi-scale memory

brief_justification: Both concern power-law / long-memory spectra and both borrow a turbulence-cascade analogy, but the core machinery differs: paper A analytically derives a universal scale-invariance CONDITION for when a spectrum becomes 1/f (a scale-free band bounded by two frequency cutoffs, plus intermittency for alpha!=1), whereas paper B is an empirical multivariate variance-ratio / covariance-eigenstructure factor DECOMPOSITION of equity returns. The overlap is thematic (long-memory power laws), not a role-for-role correspondence of the driving engine, so a generative spectral condition and a cross-sectional estimation model are not the same method.
